title: Fitting a floating floor threshold bar between rooms
subtitle: Install a T-bar, overlap, or reducer threshold strip to bridge the expansion gap where two floors meet at a doorway
excerpt: How to fit a threshold bar where a floating floor meets another floor type at a doorway. Covers choosing the right bar profile, cutting to length, securing the track to the subfloor, and clipping the cover strip.
sourceNotes: UK flooring trade practice. Manufacturer installation guides for laminate and LVT flooring systems (generic).

glossaryTerms.definition: The deliberate gap (typically 10–12 mm) left between the edge of a floating floor and every fixed obstruction: walls, door frames, pipe collars, and adjacent floors. Floating floors expand and contract with humidity and temperature; without the gap the floor can buckle. The gap is covered at walls by skirting board or beading and at doorways by a threshold bar.

content.text: at a doorway where two floors meet. Choose the bar shape for the height difference, cut to width, fix the base track, then clip the cover on top.

Three profiles cover most situations. A T-bar bridges two floors at the same level with the gap running down the centre; used where two floating floors meet or a floating floor meets a hard tile at the same height. A reducer has one sloped face; used where the floating floor is higher than an adjacent floor or tile. An overlap bar covers the edge of the floating floor where it meets a fixed floor: the bar sits on top of both surfaces. Match the profile to the actual height difference and gap position.

Measure the doorway width from reveal to reveal. Cut the bar to length with a fine-toothed saw or the mitre saw. Most aluminium threshold bars cut cleanly with a hacksaw; some snap-in plastic ones cut with a utility knife scored three times and snapped. The bar should fit snug between the door stops with no play left to right.

Most threshold bars come in two parts: a base track and a clip-on or screw-on cover strip. Slide the base track into the doorway gap, centred on the expansion gap. On a concrete or tile subfloor, drill and plug then screw the track at 300 mm centres. On a timber subfloor, screw directly without plugging. On a self-adhesive base track, clean the subfloor and press down firmly. The track must sit level (shim with card if the subfloor is uneven.

Press the cover strip down onto the base track with the heel of the hand along its length, or tap along with a rubber mallet and a timber offcut as a buffer. The cover should clip firmly with no movement or lift. Some bars screw through the cover into the track) use the screws provided. Check the bar cannot be lifted or shifted by a foot catch: any movement means the fixings are not adequate.

attrs.symptom: The threshold bar rocks or lifts at one end
attrs.cause: The subfloor is not level at the doorway, or the base track fixings are too widely spaced.
attrs.fix: Add fixings at 150 mm centres under the rocking section. If the subfloor is uneven, shim the track and re-fix.
